description: Fix scaffolded plugin example.ts to use template literals instead of string concatenation, eliminating a Biome useTemplate lint info on freshly generated code.

userStories.title: Use template literals in scaffolded example.ts
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want the scaffolded example.ts to use template literals instead of string concatenation so that freshly generated code produces zero Biome lint findings.
acceptanceCriteria: The generateExampleTool function in platform/cli/src/scaffold.ts produces example.ts code that uses template literals instead of string concatenation for both the description field and the handle return value
Running `npx @opentabs-dev/create-plugin test-lint --domain example.com && cd test-lint && npm install && npx biome lint src/` produces zero errors and zero info findings on the generated code
The generated description field uses a template literal like `Description text with ${displayName} embedded` instead of 'text' + displayName + 'more text'
The generated handle function return value uses a template literal like `Hello from ${displayName}: ${params.message}` instead of concatenation
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/scaffold.ts, lines 433-456 (generateExampleTool function).

The function currently generates code using string concatenation:
```
description: 'An example tool for ' + ${escaped} + ' — replace with your own implementation',
...
return { result: 'Hello from ' + ${escaped} + ': ' + params.message };
```

The `escaped` variable comes from `singleQuote(displayName)` which wraps the display name in single quotes with escaping. For template literal generation, you need to instead embed the display name directly into a backtick-delimited template string.

The fix is to change the code generation to emit template literals. Since the generated code uses backticks, and this function already uses backtick template literals for the outer string, you'll need to escape the inner backticks in the generated output (use \` for the generated template literals).

For the description (pure string, no runtime expression), template literal is optional but keeps consistency. The handle return value is the critical one — it mixes a literal with `params.message` and Biome flags it.

The display name may contain single quotes (the singleQuote helper handles escaping). For template literals in generated code, you need to escape `$` and backticks in the display name. The simplest approach: use a variable interpolation approach where the display name is already a string constant, and the template literal references it. Or, since the display name is known at scaffold time, embed it directly in the template literal (escaping any backticks or `${` sequences in the display name).

Simplest correct approach: instead of `singleQuote`, just embed the display name directly into generated template literal strings, escaping any backticks and `${` in the display name value. The description line becomes:
`description: \`An example tool for ${escapedForTemplate} — replace with your own implementation\`,`
And the handle return becomes:
`return { result: \`Hello from ${escapedForTemplate}: \${params.message}\` };`

Note: `\${params.message}` in the outer template string will produce `${params.message}` in the generated code (the `$` is escaped in the generation template).
